source.definition: to move steadily and continuously in a particular direction, like water or another liquid

explanation.sourceText: The English verb "flow" comes from Old English "flowan", related to the movement of liquids and other substances.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „flow“ provine din engleza veche „flowan“, fiind legat de mișcarea lichidelor și a altor substanțe.

source.additional: An irregular verb. Commonly used to describe the movement of water or liquids, also used metaphorically.

target.definition: a se mișca într-o direcție specifică; precum apa sau un lichid

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb "a curge" derives from the Latin "currere" (to run, to flow), describing the movement of liquids or time.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a curge“ derivă din latină, de la „currere“ (a alerga, a curge), descriind mișcarea lichidelor sau a timpului.

target.additional: Regular verb of the 2nd conjugation (-e). Frequently used in everyday language.

examples.sourceText: The river flows through the valley.

learningTips.text: Think of the English word "flow" as water moving smoothly and continuously, just like the Romanian verb "a curge".
Remember that "curge" sounds a bit like "courage" – imagine having the courage to keep moving forward like a flowing river.
The English word "flow" and Romanian "a curge" both describe movement, so picture a river flowing to connect them.
To recall "a curge", think of the English phrase "current flow" – "curent" means current in Romanian, linking the words.
